coredata.dc:title: Nano-structure and characterization of carbon composite with Al3+ and Mn4+ co-doped Li4Ti5O12 as anodes for Li-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Shuai
Wang, Gang
Zhang, Yiming
Xue, Caihong
Chen, Huiyuan
Yang, Guijun
Nan, Hui
Wei, Haomin
Lin, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) has several disadvantages as active material for Li-ion batteries (LIBs) such as low conductivity and poor capacity. To overcome these drawbacks and specifically to improve electrical performance of LTO, we used solvothermal method to synthesis LTO and to dope it simultaneously with Al and Mn. Doped LTO was then incorporated into composites containing graphene (G), carbon nanotube (CNT) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance specific capacity and conductivity of LIB anodes containing these composites as active materials. To have a further study of different carbon materials composite with doped LTO, these materials we obtained were thoroughly characterized. LTO doping and incorporation into composites not only increased its specific capacity and also decreased its impedance. Al3+ and Mn4+ substituted Ti4+ located at 16 d sites. First cycle specific capacity values and impedance values of anodes consisting of composites containing doped LTO and G, CNT or CQDs were 261.7, 286.2 and 296.5 mA h/g (at C/10 rate) and 13.99, 8.28, 18.79 Ω, respectively. Capacity retention after 100 cycles for these anodes was 84.6, 90.67 and 83.4%. LTO doping by Al3+ and Mn4+ and combining it with G, CNT or CQDs effectively improved its electrochemical performance. The best performance was obtained for anodes containing composites consisting of a combination of doped LTO and CNT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium titanate
Graphene
Carbon nanotube
Carbon quantum dots
Coating

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the project of basic research for application from Qinghai Science &Technology Department ( 2017-ZJ-794 ); the third batch support project of innovation and entrepreneurship training by Qinghai University ( 2018-FC-5 ); the special project of transformation of science &technology achievements from Qinghai Science &Technology Department ( 2017-GX-106 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing synthesis of LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs.

(a) XRD patterns of pure LTO, LTO-Al/Mn, LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs samples; (b) Enlarged (1 1 1) peaks of samples, (c) XRD peak corresponding to CQDs: enlarged from the weak peak marked in (a) with an asterisk “*”.
HR-TEM micrographs and elemental mapping images of (a) LTO-Al/Mn-G; (b) LTO-Al/Mn-CNT; (c) LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs.
Contact angles of (a) LTO, (b) LTO-Al/Mn-G, (c) LTO-Al/Mn-CNT, (d) LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs.
(a) Charge/discharge curves in the first cycle; (b) Cycling stability of the samples; (c) C-rate performance of anodes containing undoped LTO, LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs.
(a) Nyquist plots of LIBs containing undoped LTO, LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs samples; (b) The relationship curve between Zre and ω−1/2 in the low frequency region.
Internal resistances (Rs) and charge transfer resistances (Rct) as well as Li+ diffusion coefficients (DLi+) extrapolated from the EIS curves.

head.ce:title: Nano-structure and characterization of carbon composite with Al3+ and Mn4+ co-doped Li4Ti5O12 as anodes for Li-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) has several disadvantages as active material for Li-ion batteries (LIBs) such as low conductivity and poor capacity. To overcome these drawbacks and specifically to improve electrical performance of LTO, we used solvothermal method to synthesis LTO and to dope it simultaneously with Al and Mn. Doped LTO was then incorporated into composites containing graphene (G), carbon nanotube (CNT) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance specific capacity and conductivity of LIB anodes containing these composites as active materials. To have a further study of different carbon materials composite with doped LTO, these materials we obtained were thoroughly characterized. LTO doping and incorporation into composites not only increased its specific capacity and also decreased its impedance. Al3+ and Mn4+ substituted Ti4+ located at 16 d sites. First cycle specific capacity values and impedance values of anodes consisting of composites containing doped LTO and G, CNT or CQDs were 261.7, 286.2 and 296.5 mA h/g (at C/10 rate) and 13.99, 8.28, 18.79 Ω, respectively. Capacity retention after 100 cycles for these anodes was 84.6, 90.67 and 83.4%. LTO doping by Al3+ and Mn4+ and combining it with G, CNT or CQDs effectively improved its electrochemical performance. The best performance was obtained for anodes containing composites consisting of a combination of doped LTO and CNT.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) with spinel structure is a very promising material offering long performance life when used as active anode material for lithium ion batteries (LIBs). It demonstrates zero-strain, which ensures minimum change of its specific capacity during long-term cycling. LTO volume change during charging and discharging is significantly below 1%. In addition, it possesses flat potential plateau (at ∼1.55 V versus Li/Li+) positioned above reduction potential of most organic electrolytes [1,2], which minimizes and even completely prevents Li dendrite growth. Such flat potential plateau can also be used as an indication of a complete charging of the battery. Nevertheless, LTO has several disadvantages: its theoretical specific capacity and intrinsic conductivity are low (only 175 mA h g−1 and 10−13 S/cm [3–7], respectively). These drawbacks significantly hinder widespread commercial applications of LTO as active materials for LIB anodes.

To resolve these drawbacks and to expand usage of LTO for large-scale applications, numerous attempts were undertaken to enhance LTO performance, such as synthesis of nano-LTO [8–12], LTO doping with Na+, Ni+, Mg2+, Fe3+, V5+, Ag+, Co2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mo6+, Br− and F− by substituting Li, Ti, or O or coating LTO with a high capacity or high conductivity material such as Cu, Ag or nano-carbon [13–28]. Several specific examples include synthesis of carbon-coated N-doped nano-LTO using refluxing and microwave-assisted hydrothermal techniques. The resulting composite showed reversible capacity equal to 194 mA h g−1 at 1 C when used as an active anode material in LIBs [21]. Another example is preparation of Cu-doped Li4Ti5O12/graphene composite with an outstanding specific capacity equal to 177.3 mA h g−1 even after 100 cycles at 1C charge/discharge rate [22]. Even at 20C cycling rate, high specific capacity (equal to ∼160.6 mA h g−1) was still obtained.

Ionic radii of Al3+ and Mn4+ are very close to ionic radius of Ti4+ [29]. Thus, Al3+ and Mn4+ can substitute Ti4+ in LTO, at the same time, and improve its conductivity without significantly changing its original crystal structure, thus, retaining high cycling performance of LTO as active LIB anode material. Comparing with commercial graphite, graphene (G), a single-layer carbon material with high specific surface area, provides fast Li+ migration rate when incorporated into LIB anode as a conductive additive. Other carbon-based materials are carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which offer fast and directed transport for Li+ as well as storage, and zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which provide huge Li+ storage capacity because of their vast specific surface area and abundant pores. Thus, compounds mentioned above can improve electrical properties of LTO especially. Therefore, in this work, we incorporated Al3+ and Mn4+ into Li4Ti5O12 and then formed a composite material with G, CNT or CQDs with the goal to simultaneously improve conductivity, capacity and cycling performance of LIB anodes containing these composites as active materials.

Initial materials, 5–10 nm TiO2, Al(NO3)3∙9H2O, MnO2, G, CNT and LiOH∙H2O, were all of AR purity grade and purchased from Aladdin Inc. CQDs was prepared in the laboratory. And the initial materials are powder product. Other chemicals used in this work were span-80 and tween-80 surfactants, ethylene glycol (EG) and 30 wt % NH3∙H2O.

To prepare LTO, span-80 and tween-80 were first mixed together, after which hydrophile-lipophile balance (HLB) value of the mixture was adjusted to 9.5. Then, EG, mixed with deionized (DI) water in equal portions, was added, after which the whole mixture was placed in a 50 °C water bath and stirred for 5 min. Then, carbon material (0.0500 g) was added to the mixture and the ultrasound was used to disperse the carbon phases by an ultrasonic cell disruption system. Then, 1.525 g of TiO2, 0.0469 g of MnO2 and 0.0109 g of Al(NO3)3∙9H2O were added, and the whole system was stirred while kept in a 50 °C water bath. After 5 min of stirring, the beaker was removed from the water bath, and LiOH∙H2O was added. The mixture was stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min on a magnetic stirrer, after which it was transferred to polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lining. 10 mL of 30 wt % NH3∙H2O was then added, after which the reaction kettle was sealed and placed in a drying oven at 170 °C for 36 h. The resulting materials were washed with DI water and anhydrous ethanol for three times, respectively. The samples were dried at 50 °C for 10 h in a vacuum oven. Then, the samples were ground and transferred to a corundum crucible.

The resulting LTO samples were placed into a tube furnace, heated to 800 °C at 10 °C/min and held at this temperature for 3 h, after which they were allowed to cool naturally, with N2 protection in the whole processes. The resulting samples were then ground again using wet ball-milling, which was performed in a zirconia jar at 250 rpm for 12 h. After ball-milling, powders were rinsed with anhydrous ethanol three times and dried in vacuum oven at 50 °C for 10 h. The resulting composites, containing Al- and Mn- co-doped LTO covered with G, CNT or CQDs were named as LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs, respectively. Diagram shown in Fig. 1 graphically demonstrates step-by-step procedure described above.

Samples were characterized by using contact angle measurement (CAM, JC2000C1), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) equipped with an EMSA/MAS energy dispersive spectroscope (EDS), and X-ray diffractometry (XRD, D/max2500PC).

Standard CR2032-type half cells were assembled using lithium foil acting as a cathode and as a current collector. LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs composites were used as anode materials. Celgard 2400 was used as a separator. 1 M LiPF6 dissolved in a 1:1:1 vol % mixture of dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) and ethylene carbonate (EC) was used as electrolyte. Anodes were prepared by mixing 80 wt % of active material with 10 wt % of acetylene black and 10 wt % of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF). 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) was then added to the powder mixture to prepare slurry. The resultant slurry was then coated on aluminum foil Sheets as-prepared anodes (with the thickness of active material of ∼40 μm) and was dried in vacuum at 120 °C for 12 h. The cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box where both moisture and oxygen content were less than 0.1 ppm. The charge/discharge cycles were performed at room temperature. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured on an electrochemical workstation (Zennium E, Zahner, Germany). The charge/discharge measurements were conducted on a multichannel battery test system (Arbin BT2000, America).

XRD patterns of LTO and its composites obtained in this work showed patterns corresponding to spinel-structured LTO according to the JCPDS card number 49-0207 (see Fig. 2 (a) and (b)). XRD peaks associated with carbon materials were observed at 26.6° (see Fig. 2(a) and (c)), which confirms successful formation of LTO-carbon composites. No other peaks were detected. The peaks of the doped samples shifted to higher 2θ degrees (see enlarged (111) peaks shown in Fig. 2(b)) due to the lattice distortions caused by insertion of Al3+ and Mn4+ into LTO lattice. The maximum shift was observed for the LTO-Al/Mn sample. Lattice parameters of undoped LTO, LTO-Al/Mn, LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs calculated based on their corresponding XRD data were 8.3612, 8.3188, 8.3595, 8.3585 and 8.3553 Å, respectively. Lattice parameter decreased for Al3+ and Mn4+ doped samples relative to an undoped one because ionic radii of Al3+ and Mn4+ (equal to 0.535 and 0.530 Å, respectively) are slightly smaller than of Ti4+ (equal to 0.605 Å). Thus, substitution of Ti4+ located at 16 d sites by Al3+ and Mn4+ was successful.

To further study the morphology of the materials, high-resolution (HR) TEM images and elemental mapping images provide further illustration. Fig. 3 (a) exhibits the TEM image and elemental mapping images of LTO-Al/Mn-G, showing the (1 1 1) lattice of LTO-Al/Mn and the (0 0 2) lattice of G. The image indicates that the composite of LTO-Al/Mn-G was successfully synthesized. An elemental mapping test was conducted to further investigate the elemental information of LTO-Al/Mn-G. The distribution of the Al, Mn and C are consistent with that of Ti and O, proving that Al and Mn are uniformly doped in the LTO crystal structure and the G was coated uniformly at the surface. Fig. 3(b) and (c) exhibit the TEM images and elemental mapping images of LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs. From the images, we can clearly observe the CNT, CQDs and LTO-Al/Mn, showing that the composites were successfully obtained. The distribution of Al, Mn and C can be seen in the elemental mapping images, which is the powerful evidence for proving the uniform doping of the elements in the LTO crystal structure and the composites were finally obtained. The results are consistent with the XRD patterns.

Wettability of the sample is an important property of an electrode material. The contact between electrode materials and electrolyte will be better if electrolyte wets the surface very well. One of the most important indexes of wettability is the contact angle between the surface of electrode and the electrolyte. However, typical LIBs electrolytes are not stable in air. Thus, we used just EC, DMC and EMC (1:1:1 vol %) mixture. The sample film was prepared under the pressure of 25 Mpa. Contact angle between electrolyte and surface of LTO-Al/Mn-CNT was the smallest (see Fig. 4 ), indicating the best contact with the electrolyte, which is very beneficial for fast and efficient Li+ transport and diffusion.

First charge-discharge curves of anodes containing undoped LTO and LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs composites as active materials at C/10 current rate in the 1.0–3.0 V potential range showed behavior typical for LTO-based anodes: a flat plateau at ∼1.55 V (see Fig. 5 (a)), which is formed because of Li+ migration between Li7Ti5O12 and Li4Ti5O12 phases during battery discharging and charging, respectively, which can be presented as follows:
(1)
[
Li

]

O

32 e

Specific capacities of anodes containing as-synthesized LTO and LTO-Al/Mn-G, LTO-Al/Mn-CNT and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs composites as active materials were 169.4, 261.7, 286.2 and 296.5 mA h g−1, respectively. Comparing with undoped LTO, anodes containing composites as active materials showed significantly higher initial capacities. The highest capacity values were obtained for anodes containing LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs and LTO-Al/Mn-CNT composites as active materials, which can be explained by numerous Li+ storage sites available on CNT and CQDs [30], which results in higher energy storage performance. Doped LTO can also provide additional sites to store Li+: MnO2 can crystallize in several polymorphic modifications because of various MnO6 octahedral arrangements. This property allows the formation of composite material with host lattice that favors the additional insertion of Li+ ions into the LTO lattice which in turn exhibits higher capacity [31]. On the other side, doping of LTO with Al3+ improves reversible capacity and rate capability of the anode because Al3+ has higher conductivity. In addition, Al-O bonds are significantly more stable than the corresponding Ti-O bonds. It was reported that better stability provides enhanced Li+ diffusion in active materials with spinel structure [32]. Capacity increase observed for our composites is very promising for expanding LTO application as active material for LIB anodes.

2C cycling performance of the coin cells containing anodes with our composites was very stable (see Fig. 5 (b). The best stability was obtained for the LIB anode containing LTO-Al/Mn-CNT as active material: capacity equal to 245.8 mA h g−1 was retained even at 100th cycle. The excellent cycling performance of LTO-Al/Mn-CNT is due to the stable of the tubular structure.

High rate performance is very critical characteristic of LIBs because it determines how fast the battery can be charged and discharged. Capacities of batteries containing LTO-Al/Mn-CNT as active anode material were the highest out of all other anodes tested in this work: they varied from 278.4 to 183.1 mA h g−1 during the charge/discharge rates were changed step-wise C/2 to 7 C (see Fig. 5(c)). Capacities of anodes containing LTO-Al/Mn-G and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs composites were 174.0 and 148.3 mA h g−1 at 7 C. Capacity of anode containing LTO-Al/Mn-CNT composite recovered from 183.1 to 261.9 mA h g−1 after the current rate was returned from 7C to C/2 demonstrating only 5.9% decrease relative to its initial capacity.

EIS is considered an excellent test method to analyze and predict LIB behavior. Thus, in this work we performed EIS after the first charge/discharge cycle (see Fig. 6 ). EIS spectra for all anodes tested in this work demonstrated one semicircle and a linear part at high and low frequency ranges, respectively. High frequency processes are typically associated with charge-transfer reaction at the electrolyte/active material interface. The low-frequency region reflects processes associated with Li+ diffusion (also called Warburg diffusion) into the active material. These impedance spectra were fitted by Zview software using an equivalent circuit mode, shown in the inset of the Fig. 6(a). Rs and Rct represent the internal resistances and charge transfer resistances, respectively. CPE represents the double-layer capacitance and passivation film capacitance. Zw represents the Warburg impedance. Out of all our anodes, the one containing LTO-Al/Mn-G as active material showed the smallest semicircle (see Fig. 6).

Contribution of Warburg diffusion to the overall impedance value can be used to determine Li+ diffusion coefficient DLi of an electrode [33,34] based on the following formula:
(2)
D
L i +
=
1
2
[
(
V
m
F A
σ
w
)
d E
d X
]
2
where Vm is molar volume of Li4Ti5O12 equal to 45.73 cm3/mol, F is the Faraday constant, A is electrolyte/electrode interface area, and σw is the Warburg coefficient obtained from the EIS curves. Values of σw can be calculated by averaging slope values of the Zˊ plotted as function of square root of angular frequency ω observed in the Warburg region using the following equation:
(3)
Z ′
= R +
σ
W
ω
− 1
2

The relationship curve between Zre and ω−1/2 in the low frequency region is as shown in Fig. 6(b). dE/dx obtained from the slope of the discharge curve is equal to −2.78. Values of the internal resistances (Rs) and Li+ diffusion coefficients (DLi+) of our anodes are shown in Table 1 .

It is obvious that LTO-Al/Mn-CNT shows a minimum charge transfer resistances (8.28 Ω) and all the composites is smaller than that of pure LTO. DLi+ of LTO-Al/Mn-G is 1.04 × 10−8 cm2 s−1, which is three orders of magnitude higher than that of the other samples LTO (1.06 × 10−11 cm2 s−1), LTO-Al/Mn-CNT (3.83 × 10−11 cm2 s−1) and LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs (4.03 × 10−11 cm2 s−1).

We successfully synthesized composites based on doped LTO and various carbon materials (graphene, carbon quantum dots and carbon nanotubes). Doping with Al3+ and Mn4+ occurred by subtitling Ti4+ located at 16 d sites of LTO. Relative to undoped LTO without carbon coating, specific capacity and charge/discharge rate of the anodes containing composite materials significantly increased while impedance decreased. Thus, doping LTO significantly improved its electrochemical properties, while presence of carbon-based coating on LTO significantly improved its cycling capacity. Different carbon materials used in this work provided various advantages to the performance of the corresponding anodes: composites containing graphene demonstrated fast Li+ migration rate and high theoretical specific capacity. CQDs, because of their large surface area, provided numerous Li+ storage sites, which provided excellent specific capacity of the corresponding electrode. However, cycling stability and performance of this active material was unsatisfactory because of structural instability during repeated charge and discharge processes. CNT provided composites with all: storage capacity and structural stability. Thus, simultaneous doping of LTO and coating it with carbon-based material significantly benefits its electrochemical performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the project of basic research for application from Qinghai Science &Technology Department (2017-ZJ-794); the third batch support project of innovation and entrepreneurship training by Qinghai University (2018-FC-5); the special project of transformation of science &technology achievements from Qinghai Science &Technology Department (2017-GX-106).
